author: @justinw1765
text:
Completely unrelated to the video, but I have a question that you might have some insight to.  If I make a bath of highly concentrated, hot potassium silicate-water solution and float a thin piece of soda lime (or borosilicate) glass on the top of it which is wrapped on the top only with aluminum sheet or foil, and then I apply a strong electrical current to the bath and aluminum..would  the positively charged potassium ions tend to try to migrate into and through the glass to "get to" the strongly negatively charged aluminum?  I realize that glass is an electrical insulator, but I'm hoping that with a strong enough current and being in the bath of potassium silicate, and with such a strongly negative charge right above it, these might conspire to overcome the usual electrical resistance of the glass (which will not be that thick). 

  I'm trying to come up with a cheaper and easier way of chemically strengthening glass. 

   Do you think this has any merit or is pointless to even try?  If not practical or feasible, I'll probably go the more traditional method of molten potassium nitrate bath. But I know that electricity can have powerful and  unusual effects on materials.

   Thank you Robert, and cheers.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: If the voltage is high enough everything becomes a conductor - current - or amps won't have a lot to do with it - that will equate only to the number of coulombs required to complete the reaction you are interested in - but that also can be a function of time

replies.author: @justinw1765
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering   Thank you for the reply and information Robert.  The only high voltage thing I have is one of those violet ray wand things. Looks like I may have to go the traditional route. 

 Thanks again

author: @JohnHoranzy
text: The face contact area is an important consideration for friction and heat generation. I wonder how that compares to a fully engineered gear system. But this is so wonderful for simple mechanisms,

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering

replies.author: @JohnHoranzy
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering You could try a stripe of Dry Erase marker on the meshing surfaces to see how it smears on the contact face. Or maybe just marks on the side faces to see if there is slip.
Thank you for sharing your knowledge with us and thank you for your smile and enthusiasm.

author: @VeniceInventors
text:
The simplicity of this approach makes it a lot more accessible than the original which requires a 3D printer or some impressive carving skills.
And acrylic looks so nice, it makes everything seem less "material" as if it's there but not really.
It would be interesting to find out if having more layers provides any benefit or not. I suspect that very smooth curves on the gears and tight tolerances may be more important than the number of discs pushing against the gear.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: to be honest i suspect it is a balance of the two mate - a few folks have suggested 3 lobes and i can see that - if you start to go too much over you are really approximating what we started with and lose the simplicity of the construction - so again it is going to be a balance

replies.author: @blairleighton3343
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkeringBut wouldn’t adding more layers in a progressive spacing add reliability and longer wear resistance?

replies.author: @TuttleScott
replies.text: @blairleighton3343 it would but this is fine for what it is. you're basically building up to a helix gear.

author: @BiscuitWaite
text: Well you're idea is far easier to machine.  The trickiest part would be the cams and that would only be difficult if you wanted it to be made from billet but a basic machinist could do it.  Another reason I would want to make it from billet is for strength which the cams as designed wouldn't have.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: good point mate - but they are just eccentrics so you could cut them from a round billet and drill off centre

author: @azlandpilotcar4450
text: Looks simple, and also like there is very little backlash.   If the two smaller driving eccentrics were bearings instead of plexi, it would also be near friction free.  Cheers!

author: @neoprints1325
text: If a gear does not have an involute tooth profile, it may experience uneven load distribution, leading to rapid wear and reduced efficiency.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Involute is just one tooth profile mate there is also cycloid - which this one is and trochoid - Involute is not 'the best' in all situations it is just the most common

author: @grendel1960a
text: for better engagement you could even add more plates, they dont even need to have the plates aligned between layers, 3 layers offset could give you a gear that wont go out of alignment (much like a herringbone gear will always stay centred) though you do have to consider the side friction between plates and the smaller gears unless you space them with washers.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: awesome thinking mate - thanks for sharing

replies.author: @motormaker
replies.text: Yes indeed.   If you need to transfer more torque…add more plates and more cams.   The modular nature of this design makes it easily adaptable for many uses.

What is the backlash like though, and  how far can it be adjusted out? Thanks
